summary: Fowler addresses the recurring question of whether DSLs can enable business people to write software without programmers, applying what he calls the 'COBOL inference' - the observation that COBOL aimed for the same goal and failed. He argues that programming requires a particular mindset for giving precise machine instructions and structuring complex programs, which is why disintermediation attempts consistently fail. However, he identifies a 'sweet spot': DSLs should be business-readable rather than business-writable, creating a rich communication channel between developers and domain experts. While business-writable DSLs are possible, the cost of creating proper editing environments and tooling is significantly higher.
key_points: The 'COBOL inference': attempts to eliminate programmers from coding have consistently failed throughout computing history
Programming requires a specific mindset for precise instruction-giving and structuring complex systems that resists disintermediation
The sweet spot for DSLs is business-readable rather than business-writable - programmers write code that business people can understand and review
Business-writable DSLs are possible but require expensive tooling investment for editing, error messages, debugging and testing
Spreadsheets are the notable exception to the COBOL inference, successfully enabling non-programmers to run significant business functions
money_quotes: I like to apply the COBOL inference to this line of thought. That is that one of the original aims of COBOL was to allow people to write software without programmers, and we know how that worked out.

I do think that programming involves a particular mind-set, an ability to both give precise instructions to a machine and the ability to structure a large amount of such instructions to make a comprehensible program.
The sweet spot, however is in making DSLs business-readable rather than business-writable.
If business people are able to look at the DSL code and understand it, then we can build a deep and rich communication channel between software development and the underlying domain.
It's also why many 'non-programming' environments end up breeding their own class of programmers-in-fact.
All over the world suprisingly big business functions are run off the back of Excel. Serious programmers tend to look down their noses at these, but we need to take them more seriously.
key_insight: The value of DSLs lies not in eliminating programmers but in creating readable artifacts that bridge the communication gap between technical and business domains.
